Response:
You’ve nailed the tension—AI’s a double-edged sword, and I’ve felt that duality in my own projects. Let me share a concrete example: last semester, I used an AI tool to generate code for a game prototype. It handled mechanics, optimized collision detection, and even suggested creative UI elements I hadn’t considered. But the reflection kicked in when I realized its “optimizations” ignored accessibility—assuming all players used a mouse, not touch controls or keyboards. I had to rework those parts, which made me confront how easy it is to overlook assumptions when relying on AI.  

The balance feels like having a co-pilot great at navigation but forgetful of the fuel gauge. You trust the tool, but you’re always asking, *“What’s the bigger picture?”* It’s not about rejecting AI—it’s using it as a lens to expand your thinking, not replace it.  

I’ve caught myself getting too reliant on it too. There’s dry humor in watching an AI generate a perfectly functional but culturally insensitive meme generator, then realizing I spent 30 minutes tweaking the code instead of questioning the premise. That’s the beauty of the tension—it forces you to stay critical.  

So, how do you navigate that balance in your work? Do you ever find yourself in a “trust the tool” vs. “question everything” loop? 🎮🤖
